criteria: The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the overall Swin UNETR architecture including the encoder, decoder, and their connections via skip connections.
The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the hierarchical Swin Transformer encoder including patch partition, window partitioning, shifted window mechanism, and patch merging.
The agent has read Equations 1 and 2 to understand the W-MSA, SW-MSA, MLP, LN computations and the scaled dot-product attention formula in the Swin Transformer block.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand the CNN-based decoder design with residual blocks, deconvolutional upsampling, and skip connections at each resolution stage.
The agent has read Section 3.3 completely to understand the soft Dice loss function formulation.
The agent has read Section 3.4 completely to understand all implementation details including training hyperparameters, data augmentation strategies, and sliding window inference settings.
The agent has read Section 3.5 completely to understand the BraTS 2021 dataset, five-fold cross-validation scheme, and 10-model ensembling strategy.

The agent has read Table 1 to check the Swin UNETR model configurations: embed dimension 768, feature size 48, number of blocks [2,2,2,2], window size [7,7,7], number of heads [3,6,12,24], 61.98M parameters, and 394.84G FLOPs.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the five-fold cross-validation benchmark results for Swin UNETR, nnU-Net, SegResNet, and TransBTS across ET, WT, and TC sub-regions.
The agent has read Tables 3 and 4 to check BraTS 2021 validation and testing dataset benchmark results in terms of mean Dice score and Hausdorff distance.
The plan includes implementing the Swin UNETR architecture as a U-shaped network with a Swin Transformer encoder connected to a CNN-based decoder via skip connections at multiple resolutions.

The plan includes implementing the patch partition layer that creates 3D non-overlapping patches of size 2×2×2 from the 4-channel MRI input and projects them into an embedding space of dimension C=48.
The plan includes implementing the shifted window mechanism with window size [7,7,7] and 3D cyclic shifting by (⌊M/2⌋, ⌊M/2⌋, ⌊M/2⌋) voxels in alternating layers.
The plan includes implementing the 4-stage Swin Transformer encoder with [2,2,2,2] blocks per stage and attention heads [3,6,12,24] per stage.
The plan includes implementing the patch merging layer that groups 2×2×2 patches, concatenates them into 4C-dimensional embeddings, and reduces dimensionality to 2C via a linear layer.
The plan includes implementing the CNN-based decoder with residual blocks containing two 3×3×3 convolutional layers normalized by instance normalization.
The plan includes implementing skip connections that feed encoder feature representations at each of the 5 resolution stages (i ∈ {0,1,2,3,4}) and the bottleneck (i=5) into the decoder.
The plan includes implementing the soft Dice loss function as defined in Equation 3, computed voxel-wise over J classes.
The plan includes implementing the BraTS 2021 data loading pipeline with 4 MRI modalities (T1, T1Gd, T2, T2-FLAIR) as a 4-channel 240×240×155 input volume.

The plan includes all data preprocessing and augmentation steps: zero-mean unit-std normalization over non-zero voxels, random 128×128×128 patch cropping, random axis mirror flip, per-channel intensity shift, and per-channel intensity scale.
The plan includes training for 800 epochs with learning rate 0.0008, linear warmup, and cosine annealing LR scheduler with batch size 1 per GPU.
The plan includes implementing five-fold cross-validation with an 80:20 ratio on the 1251 BraTS 2021 training cases.
The plan includes implementing sliding window inference with an overlap of 0.7 for neighboring voxels.
The plan includes implementing an ensemble of 10 Swin UNETR models from two separate five-fold cross-validation training runs.
The plan includes implementing the baseline models (nnU-Net, SegResNet, TransBTS) for comparison in the five-fold cross-validation experiments.
The code for the patch partition layer has been implemented to divide the input volume X ∈ R^{H×W×D×S} into non-overlapping 3D tokens of size 2×2×2, resulting in tokens of dimension H/2 × W/2 × D/2.
The code for the linear embedding layer has been implemented to project the 3D patch tokens (with raw feature dimension 2×2×2×4=32) into an embedding space of dimension C=48.
The code for non-overlapping window partitioning has been implemented with window size M×M×M=[7,7,7] to partition 3D token grids into local windows for self-attention computation.
The code for 3D cyclic shifting has been implemented to shift feature maps by (⌊M/2⌋, ⌊M/2⌋, ⌊M/2⌋) voxels for efficient computation of the shifted window self-attention (SW-MSA).
The W-MSA (Window Multi-head Self-Attention) module has been implemented as per Equation 1: z_hat^l = W-MSA(LN(z^{l-1})) + z^{l-1}, z^l = MLP(LN(z_hat^l)) + z_hat^l.
The SW-MSA (Shifted Window Multi-head Self-Attention) module has been implemented as per Equation 1 for layer l+1: z_hat^{l+1} = SW-MSA(LN(z^l)) + z^l, z^{l+1} = MLP(LN(z_hat^{l+1})) + z_hat^{l+1}.
The scaled dot-product attention has been implemented as Equation 2: Attention(Q,K,V) = Softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d))V, where d is the size of the query and key.

The complete Swin Transformer block has been implemented with the paired W-MSA and SW-MSA structure (Equation 1), where consecutive layers l and l+1 alternate between regular and shifted window self-attention.
The 4-stage hierarchical encoder has been implemented with [2,2,2,2] Swin Transformer blocks per stage, producing feature maps at resolutions H/2, H/4, H/8, and H/16 with total L=8 layers.
The number of attention heads for the 4 encoder stages has been correctly set to [3,6,12,24] as specified in Table 1.

The patch merging layer has been implemented to group 2×2×2 neighboring patches (producing 4C-dimensional concatenated features), then reduce to 2C via a linear layer, halving the spatial resolution between encoder stages.
The decoder feature extraction has been implemented to reshape encoder output feature representations at each stage i ∈ {0,1,2,3,4} into spatial tensors of shape H/2^i × W/2^i × D/2^i.
The residual block in the decoder has been implemented with two sequential 3×3×3 convolutional layers, each followed by instance normalization.
The deconvolutional upsampling layer has been implemented in the decoder to increase the spatial resolution of feature maps by a factor of 2.

Skip connections have been implemented to concatenate the encoder feature representations from each stage (0 through 4 and the bottleneck stage 5) with the corresponding upsampled decoder feature maps before feeding into a residual block.

The final segmentation head has been implemented as a 1×1×1 convolutional layer followed by a sigmoid activation function, producing 3 output channels corresponding to the ET, WT, and TC tumor sub-regions.

The soft Dice loss has been implemented as per Equation 3: L(G,Y) = 1 - (2/J) * sum_{j=1}^{J} [sum_{i=1}^{I} G_{i,j} * Y_{i,j} / (sum_{i=1}^{I} G_{i,j}^2 + sum_{i=1}^{I} Y_{i,j}^2)], computed voxel-wise across all J classes.
The BraTS 2021 data loading pipeline has been implemented to load 4 MRI modalities (T1, T1Gd, T2, T2-FLAIR) as a single 4-channel 3D volume of size 240×240×155.
The image normalization has been implemented to compute zero mean and unit standard deviation statistics computed only over non-zero voxels for each input channel.
The random 128×128×128 patch cropping data augmentation has been implemented for sampling training patches from the full 3D MRI volumes.
The random axis mirror flip augmentation has been implemented with probability 0.5, applied independently for all 3 spatial axes.
The random per-channel intensity shift augmentation has been implemented with additive values sampled uniformly from the range (-0.1, 0.1).
The random per-channel intensity scale augmentation has been implemented with multiplicative values sampled uniformly from the range (0.9, 1.1).
The sliding window inference has been implemented with an overlap ratio of 0.7 between neighboring windows to handle full-volume 3D MRI segmentation at inference time.
The optimizer has been configured with a learning rate of 0.0008 for training Swin UNETR.
The learning rate scheduler has been implemented with linear warmup followed by cosine annealing over the full 800 training epochs.
The training configuration has been set with batch size 1 per GPU and training on 8 NVIDIA V100 GPUs for distributed training.
The five-fold cross-validation splits have been implemented with an 80:20 ratio on the 1251 BraTS 2021 training cases for all models.
The model ensembling has been implemented to aggregate predictions from 10 Swin UNETR models, obtained from 2 separate five-fold cross-validation training runs.
The SegResNet baseline has been implemented with its residual encoder-decoder architecture for comparison in the five-fold cross-validation experiments.
The nnU-Net baseline has been implemented with its generic U-Net architecture for comparison in the five-fold cross-validation experiments.
The TransBTS baseline has been implemented with its ViT-based architecture for comparison in the five-fold cross-validation experiments.
The evaluation metrics (mean Dice score and Hausdorff distance in mm) have been implemented for all three tumor sub-regions: ET (Enhancing Tumor), WT (Whole Tumor), and TC (Tumor Core).
The Swin UNETR model has been trained for all 5 folds of cross-validation on the BraTS 2021 training dataset and executed successfully without errors.
All three baseline models (nnU-Net, SegResNet, TransBTS) have been trained on the BraTS 2021 dataset and executed successfully without errors for 5-fold cross-validation comparison.
The sliding window inference has been executed on the BraTS 2021 validation dataset (219 cases) using the 10-model ensemble of Swin UNETR without errors.
The five-fold cross-validation evaluation has been executed to compute mean Dice scores for ET, WT, and TC sub-regions for Swin UNETR and all baseline models.
The 10-model ensemble inference has been executed on the BraTS 2021 testing dataset to produce final segmentation results.
The five-fold cross-validation Dice results of Swin UNETR match Table 2: average Dice of ET=0.891, WT=0.933, TC=0.917, and overall average=0.913 across all 5 folds.

The per-fold results in Table 2 for Swin UNETR are reproduced: Fold 1 (ET=0.876, WT=0.929, TC=0.914), Fold 2 (ET=0.908, WT=0.938, TC=0.919), Fold 3 (ET=0.891, WT=0.931, TC=0.919), Fold 4 (ET=0.890, WT=0.937, TC=0.920), Fold 5 (ET=0.891, WT=0.934, TC=0.917).
The five-fold cross-validation results confirm that Swin UNETR outperforms nnU-Net (average Dice 0.913 vs 0.908) across all five folds and all three tumor sub-regions as shown in Table 2.
The five-fold cross-validation results confirm that Swin UNETR outperforms SegResNet (average Dice 0.913 vs 0.907) across all five folds and all three tumor sub-regions as shown in Table 2.
The five-fold cross-validation results confirm that Swin UNETR outperforms TransBTS (average Dice 0.913 vs 0.891) across all five folds and all three tumor sub-regions as shown in Table 2.
The BraTS 2021 validation dataset Dice score results match Table 3: Swin UNETR achieves ET=0.858, WT=0.926, TC=0.885.
The BraTS 2021 validation dataset Hausdorff distance results match Table 3: Swin UNETR achieves ET=6.016mm, WT=5.831mm, TC=3.770mm.
The BraTS 2021 testing dataset Dice score results match Table 4: Swin UNETR achieves ET=0.853, WT=0.927, TC=0.876.
The BraTS 2021 testing dataset Hausdorff distance results match Table 4: Swin UNETR achieves ET=16.326mm, WT=4.739mm, TC=15.309mm.
